segments.text: uh, going a little further in this idea of using distillation to do things that you couldn't do before, um, is some work that I did with Graham last year this past year, um, which is called prompt model and the idea here is that.
Let's forget that distillation is anything but just like a bit as a generator.
And now, um, distillation is one way to get training data for your model, but there might be other ways to get data as well that we're like leaving on the table.

So I think the key idea here is, can we combine retrieve data, and this can data that exists on the internet with data generated from now, um, can we put these two things together and do even better. So in this in this paper, we.
Ask the user to specify their task in a prompt, kind of like what you'd use for GPT three. And, uh, they can give a couple examples if they want.

And then given this prompt, we first retrieve existing data sets that might be relevant to that prompt. So we had like a method for data set retrieval in a previous paper that just uses text to find similar data sets. So if I say, um,
answer biomandical questions about, uh,
for cancer doctors, it might find like the bio ASQ data set.

Uh, and then we take that retrieve data set, which is likely to be high quality, but may not match the task that the user actually cares about. It might be like a little bit different than what the user actually wants.
We then complement this retrieve data set with generated data generated by a language model, which is potentially like not that high quality, but is much more likely to match the user's intentions.

We then did one other thing that's not that important, which is retrieving like a pre trained model as well, like maybe you have a pre trained model that is in your domain that you want to, that you can actually benefit from.
Then we just put all these things together fine tune this small model on your generated and retrieve data sets.

And then, um, I think the really cool thing here was that we were able to obtain small models that often outperform GPT 3, even though GPT 3 was the model used to generate data. So like we were beating the teacher.
Uh, by leveraging like both distillation, but also taking advantage of existing data sets that were available on the internet.
So I think that, uh, generally this is a direction I'm really excited about distillation for the purpose of advancing model capabilities.
And, uh, I think that this kind of came at a time when distillation was becoming really popular, but now it's often used by a different name, which is called synthetic data generation.
It's effectively the same thing as hard target distillation.
